title: Build Matchmaker Lambda Function
content: Step-by-Step Implementation Step 1: Create AWS Lambda Function Navigate to AWS Lambda console -> Click Create function. Function name: FightingGameMatchmaker. Runtime: Node.js 20.x. Step 2: Configure Lambda IAM Permissions & Environment Variables Under Configuration -> Permissions, click the Execution Role link. Add an inline policy granting dynamodb:PutItem, dynamodb:GetItem, dynamodb:DeleteItem, and ec2:DescribeInstances access. Under Environment variables, click Edit and set: MATCHMAKING_QUEUE_TABLE: MatchmakingQueue ACTIVE_MATCHES_TABLE: ActiveMatches Step 3: Deploy & Test Lambda Function Paste the Matchmaker Node.js source code into the editor and click Deploy. Test payload execution for Player1 and Player2. Verify that players are successfully inserted into MatchmakingQueue.

title: Clean Up Cognito & DynamoDB
content: Step-by-Step Implementation Step 1: Delete Cognito User Pool Go to the Amazon Cognito console -> User pools. Select the User pool you created and click Delete. Confirm deletion by typing the pool name. Step 2: Delete Cognito Identity Pool Select Identity pools from the Cognito dashboard. Select the Identity pool you created and click Delete. Confirm the deletion. Step 3: Delete DynamoDB Tables Go to the Amazon DynamoDB console -> Tables. Select all the tables you created (MatchmakingQueue, ActiveMatches). Click Delete and confirm.

title: Create Cognito User Pool
content: Step-by-Step Implementation Step 1: Select AWS Region Switch your AWS Management Console region to Singapore (ap-southeast-1). Step 2: Create Cognito User Pool Search for Cognito in the AWS Console search bar. Click Get started for free. Select Single-page application (SPA) and enter the Application name: FightingGame. Under Configure options: Check Username self-registration. Select Required attributes: Select email for password recovery. Click Create user directory. User Pool ID: ap-southeast-1_phYoaMUPC

title: Create MatchmakingQueue Table
content: Step-by-Step Implementation Step 1: Open Amazon DynamoDB Console Search for DynamoDB in the AWS search bar. Click Get started. Step 2: Create Table MatchmakingQueue Click Create table. Enter Table Details: Table name: MatchmakingQueue Partition key: playerId (String) Sort key: Leave blank Table settings: Default settings Click Create table.

title: Enable DynamoDB Streams & IAM Roles
content: Step-by-Step Implementation Step 1: Enable DynamoDB Streams Navigate to DynamoDB -> Select ActiveMatches table. Under Exports and streams, enable DynamoDB stream (View type: NEW_AND_OLD_IMAGES). Step 2: Create IAM Role for Analytics Lambda Create IAM Role MatchAnalyticRole. Attach policy granting read access to DynamoDB Streams (dynamodb:GetRecords, dynamodb:GetShardIterator, dynamodb:DescribeStream, dynamodb:ListStreams).

title: Launch EC2 Base & Setup Node.js Server
content: Step-by-Step Implementation Step 1: Launch Base EC2 Instance Navigate to Amazon EC2 -> Click Launch Instance. Name: FightingGameServer. AMI: Ubuntu Server 24.04 LTS. Instance Type: t3.medium (Graviton/x86 compatible). Storage: 8 GB gp3. Step 2: Set Up Node.js Game Server Environment SSH into the EC2 instance. Install Node.js runtime and dependencies. Start the game server application and verify local port binding.

title: Setup GitHub OIDC Provider & IAM Roles
content: Step-by-Step Implementation Step 1: Configure GitHub OIDC Provider in AWS IAM Navigate to IAM -> Identity providers -> Add GitHub OIDC Provider. Create an IAM Role for GitHub Actions with trust policy restricted to Shinjiiiiiiiii/TranAnhTu-WorkShop.

title: User Authentication with Amazon Cognito
content:
Overview In this chapter, you will configure Amazon Cognito User Pools and Cognito Identity Pools to handle user sign-up, sign-in, and temporary IAM credential generation for downloading game assets.
Sub-modules 5.1.1 Create Cognito User Pool 5.1.2 Configure App Client & Identity Pool 5.1.3 Verify User Registration UI

title: Blog 1
content:
HOW ALS GEOANALYTICS’ LITHOLENS REVOLUTIONIZES CORE LOGGING THROUGH MACHINE LEARNING WITH AMAZON EKS ALS Geoanalytics developed LithoLens, a cloud-native platform utilizing computer vision and machine learning (ML) to automate and optimize the process of geological core logging. The system automates the ingestion, classification, and analysis of drill core photographs, significantly speeding up geological evaluations for mining and resource exploration.
Key Architectural Components: Client Access & API Layer: The client applications connect securely through Amazon API Gateway and AWS Lambda (API Layer), with user authentication managed via Amazon Cognito. Compute & Processing Layer: Amazon EKS (Elastic Kubernetes Service) orchestrates the containerized machine learning inference workloads. Running models on EKS allows ALS to scale container instances dynamically based on the volume of images being processed. Storage & Database Layer: Amazon S3 stores the massive volume of high-resolution drill core images. Amazon RDS manages metadata, user accounts, and structural geological records. Amazon CloudWatch monitors application performance and stores operational logs. Benefits of the Architecture: Scalability: Auto-scaling Kubernetes clusters in EKS efficiently handle spikes in machine learning workloads. Cost Efficiency: Serverless APIs (API Gateway & Lambda) combined with scalable containers ensure ALS only pays for active computing resource usage. Performance: GPU-optimized instances in EKS speed up deep learning image inference. Architecture Diagram: Links and References: Facebook Post: AWS Study Group Facebook Post Reference Article: How ALS Geoanalytics’ LithoLens revolutionizes core logging through machine learning with Amazon EKS

title: Event 1
content: Summary Report: FCAJ Community Day Event Information: Event Name: FCAJ Community Day Date: Saturday morning, May 23, 2026 Location: 26th Floor (and 36th Floor), Bitexco Financial Tower, 02 Hai Trieu Street, Ben Nghe Ward, District 1, Ho Chi Minh City Organizer: AWS Study Group (FCAJ Community) Event Objectives: Networking & Inspiration: Provide an open space for attendees to connect, inspire each other, and actively build professional networks and relationships. Knowledge Sharing: Go through 6 engaging sessions focusing on AI, Cloud, and real-world production cases. Enterprise-Grade Focus: Discuss best practices for designing scalable, production-ready system architectures for enterprises. Empowering Young Talents: Help students and junior engineers understand the shifting IT job market in the AI era, encouraging them to focus on building “real products” rather than simple demos. Speakers: Quynh Mai – Program MC Nguyen Gia Hung – Head of Solution Architecture at AWS Vietnam, Founder of FCAJ Anh Tinh – Platform Engineer at Gotam X Chi Hai Anh – Systems Engineer at Pacific Vietnam Anh Thinh – DevOps Engineer UTM Group – Hackathon participants Chi Vi – Core Systems Specialist at VBBank Key Highlights: 1. Shift in the IT Job Market As AI dramatically reduces software development costs, the demand for custom software creation will spike exponentially. This trend creates new job roles, such as platform engineering and specialized debugging of AI-generated code. 2. AI Contextual Challenges & Randomness Context is Everything: LLMs often produce generic or irrelevant output when users dump requests without proper localized context. Inference Randomness: Even with temperature set to 0, LLMs hosted on cloud systems can return varying outputs due to inference optimization layers implemented by cloud providers. 3. Startup Credit Rating Challenges Assessing creditworthiness for startups is challenging as they lack traditional collaterals or three-year financial statements. 4. Solutions Introduced Amazon CloudFront Flat-rate Pricing: A pricing structure helping businesses avoid unpredictable billing spikes when using Content Delivery Networks (CDN). Multi-Agent Systems: Deploying multiple specialized AI agents playing specific roles (e.g., financial analyst, market researcher) to solve complex business flows. Core Technologies: Amazon Bedrock Agent Core, Amazon Q (for internal agents), Amazon S3, Terraform, and AWS CloudFront. 5. Live Demos & Case Studies Amazon Q Revenue Analysis: Demo of Amazon Q automatically parsing Excel financial sheets and generating a clean, interactive dashboard. UTM Morpho Application: Demo of an AI tool converting wireframe drawings into clean HTML/CSS code, allowing live edits via a drag-and-drop UI. Comparison Case Study: Analyzing the differences in text outputs of LLMs hosted on Amazon Bedrock versus locally under temperature=0. Enterprise Security Standards: Deploying AI in enterprises requires strict guardrails, anti-prompt injection, auditing, and high-standard security compliance. Key Takeaways: 1. Design Mindset & Methodologies Always start with business questions first: “Who is using this, what are they using it for, why must they use it, and when is the right time?” Hackathon Strategy: Focus on perfecting one core feature rather than overloading the project with too many incomplete features (avoid overthinking). 2. Technical Learnings Deep dive into CloudFront architecture (VPC Origin, mTLS). Gained insights into LLM next-token selection principles (logits and probabilities). Testing: Emphasized the importance of rigorous testing with edge cases to control LLM outputs and avoid over-generation. Applying to Work: Contextual Prompting: Implement strict role configurations and contextual boundaries for development assistants to yield better code output. Tooling Experiments: Pilot Amazon Bedrock Agent Core for multi-agent building, and utilize Terraform as Infrastructure as Code (IaC) for environment automation. Security & Audits: Configure Guardrails and Audit Trails to evaluate and track AI decisions before pushing them to Production. Event Experience: Career Insights: Gained practical wisdom from speakers discussing non-traditional career paths (working without a university degree) and dealing with bank compliance. Hands-on Demos: Saw real-time UI streaming and automated dashboards generated by Amazon Q. Networking: Exchanged valuable ideas, project architectures, and contact info with other developers. Memorable Case Study: Learnt about an incident where a developer copied buggy ChatGPT code straight to a bank’s Production system, triggering a full-scale code audit. Key Lessons & Next Steps: Enterprise Standards: Enterprise systems are completely different from PoCs or school projects; they demand high security, strict compliance, and solid software engineering. Competitive Edge: Soft skills, English proficiency, practical domain knowledge, and a university degree remain vital differentiators in the job market. Future Focus: Shift learning focus from shallow AI wrappers to core Software Engineering, Backend principles, and AI adoption strategies. Event Photos:

title: Week 1 Worklog
content: Week 1 Objectives: Get introduced to Cloud Computing and the Amazon Web Services (AWS) ecosystem. Learn the differences between IaaS, PaaS, and SaaS cloud delivery models. Get familiar with the AWS Management Console interface. Create a secure AWS Account and set up IAM users, Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA), and Billing Alarms to prevent unexpected costs. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material Tue - General introduction to Cloud Computing - Understand AWS Global Infrastructure (Regions, AZs, Edge Locations) 05/05/2026 05/05/2026 AWS Global Infrastructure Wed - Learn Cloud Service Models: IaaS vs PaaS vs SaaS - Create an AWS Free Tier Account 05/06/2026 05/06/2026 AWS Cloud Service Models Thu - Get familiar with AWS Management Console - Practice: Set up IAM Identity Center, Create IAM Users, and configure MFA 05/07/2026 05/07/2026 AWS IAM Getting Started Fri - Learn AWS Billing basics - Practice: Create a Billing Alarm via CloudWatch - Set up basic IAM user group policies 05/08/2026 05/08/2026 AWS Billing Alarms Week 1 Achievements: Gained a solid understanding of Cloud Computing concepts and AWS global infrastructure. Created a secure AWS Free Tier account. Successfully set up IAM users and groups, enforcing the principle of least privilege, and enabled MFA for root and IAM accounts. Created a CloudWatch Billing Alarm to monitor and alert when usage charges exceed $5. Acquired basic knowledge on how to navigate the AWS Console and manage user permissions.

title: Worklog
content:
Typically, and as a standard, a worklog is carried out over about 3 months (throughout the internship period) with weekly contents as follows:
Week 1: Getting familiar with AWS Cloud
Week 2: Compute Services - Amazon EC2
Week 3: Storage Services - Amazon S3
Week 4: Networking on AWS - Amazon VPC
Week 5: Database Services - Amazon RDS & DynamoDB
Week 6: Serverless - AWS Lambda & API Gateway

Week 10: CI/CD and System Architecture
Week 11: Group Project Implementation
Week 12: Project Completion and Report

title: Bake AMI & Create Launch Template
content: Step-by-Step Implementation Step 1: Bake Custom AMI Tag the running server instance. Actions -> Image and templates -> Create image (FightingGameServer-AMI). Step 2: Create Launch Template for Spot Fleet Navigate to Launch Templates -> Click Create launch template: Select FightingGameServer-AMI. Set Purchasing Option: Spot Instances.

title: Clean Up Lambda & API Gateway
content: Step-by-Step Implementation Step 1: Delete Lambda Functions Go to the AWS Lambda console -> Functions. Select the Lambda functions you created (FightingGameMatchmaker, MatchAnalyticLambda). Click Actions -> Delete and confirm. Step 2: Delete API Gateway APIs Go to the Amazon API Gateway console. Select the API you created (FightingGameAPI). Click Actions -> Delete and confirm.

title: Configure App Client & Identity Pool
content: Step-by-Step Implementation Step 1: Configure App Client Navigate to Applications -> App clients. Select FightingGame app client. Under Authentication flows, enable ALLOW_USER_PASSWORD_AUTH. Save changes. App Client ID: 73ipqvvo7h3u0j3elfqlj23jo3 Step 2: Create Cognito Identity Pool Return to the Cognito dashboard and select Identity pools. Click Create identity pool. Select Authenticated access -> Amazon Cognito user pool. Under Configure permissions: Create a new IAM Role named FightingGameAuthenticatedRole. Under Connect identity providers: Enter your User Pool ID (ap-southeast-1_phYoaMUPC) and App Client ID (73ipqvvo7h3u0j3elfqlj23jo3). Name the pool: FightingGameIdentityPool. Click Create identity pool. Identity Pool ID: ap-southeast-1:a5d743b9-e4a4-45d2-9cb1-9d214cee574c

title: Create ActiveMatches Table & Verify
content: Step-by-Step Implementation Step 1: Create Table ActiveMatches Click Create table. Enter Table Details: Table name: ActiveMatches Partition key: playerId (String) Sort key: Leave blank Table settings: Default settings Click Create table. Step 2: Verify Tables Status Check the table list to ensure both MatchmakingQueue and ActiveMatches have reached Active status.

title: Create MatchAnalytic Lambda & Verify Logs
content: Step-by-Step Implementation Step 1: Create MatchAnalyticLambda Function Navigate to AWS Lambda -> Click Create function. Name: MatchAnalyticLambda. Assign MatchAnalyticRole. Step 2: Configure Event Source Mapping & Verify Analytics Output Add DynamoDB Stream trigger to MatchAnalyticLambda. Test match conclusion: when a match record transitions to finished state, MatchAnalyticLambda automatically processes player statistics and writes event logs to the analytics store.

title: Database Setup: DynamoDB Matchmaking Queue & Active Matches
content:
Overview In this chapter, you will create two core Amazon DynamoDB tables: MatchmakingQueue (for pending players) and ActiveMatches (for active match sessions).
Sub-modules 5.2.1 Create MatchmakingQueue Table 5.2.2 Create ActiveMatches Table

title: Deploy REST API & Cognito Authorizer
content: Step-by-Step Implementation Step 1: Create Amazon API Gateway REST API Navigate to API Gateway console -> Click Create API. Select REST API -> Build. API Name: FightingGameAPI. Step 2: Create Resources /join & /check Click Create resource: Resource Name: join -> Create method POST -> Link to FightingGameMatchmaker Lambda. Click Create resource: Resource Name: check -> Create method GET -> Link to FightingGameMatchmaker Lambda. Step 3: Configure Cognito Authorizer & CORS Under Authorizers, click Create authorizer: Name: FightinggameCognitoAuthorizer Type: Cognito User Pool: ap-southeast-1_phYoaMUPC Attach FightinggameCognitoAuthorizer to /join (POST) and /check (GET) method request settings. Enable CORS for both /join and /check endpoints. Step 4: Deploy API Gateway Select / root resource -> Click Deploy API. Deployment stage: prod. Invoke URL: https://6whg1d5qca.execute-api.ap-southeast-1.amazonaws.com/prod

title: Install CodeDeploy Agent on Ubuntu 24.04
content:
Step-by-Step Implementation Step 1: Install & Patch AWS CodeDeploy Agent on Ubuntu 24.04 LTS Execute the following commands on the EC2 instance to patch the Ruby 3.3 dependency compatibility issue:
# 1. Install prerequisites sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install -y ruby-full ruby-webrick wget gdebi-core # 2. Download CodeDeploy deb package cd /tmp wget https://aws-codedeploy-ap-southeast-1.s3.ap-southeast-1.amazonaws.com/releases/codedeploy-agent_1.8.1-26_all.deb # 3. Unpack and fix Ruby dependency dpkg-deb -R codedeploy-agent_1.8.1-26_all.deb /tmp/codedeploy-extracted sed -i "s/ruby3.2/ruby3.3/g" /tmp/codedeploy-extracted/DEBIAN/control dpkg-deb -b /tmp/codedeploy-extracted /tmp/codedeploy-agent_fixed.deb # 4. Install patched package and start service sudo dpkg -i /tmp/codedeploy-agent_fixed.deb sudo systemctl enable codedeploy-agent sudo systemctl start codedeploy-agent

title: Proposal
content:
Serverless & Spot Instance Backend Architecture for Live-Service Games on AWS High-Performance, Scalable, and Cost-Optimized Cloud Infrastructure for Multiplayer Games 1. Executive Summary This project presents a cloud-native backend architecture designed for live-service multiplayer games on Amazon Web Services (AWS). Instead of maintaining an expensive dedicated server fleet running 24/7 regardless of player demand, this architecture provisions compute resources dynamically only when actually needed: during player authentication, matchmaking, and active live game sessions.
All metagame components—including authentication, asset distribution, matchmaking, and post-match analytics—operate on a 100% Serverless architecture. The live game server sessions run within an EC2 Spot Fleet inside a dedicated, isolated VPC network boundary and are spun up dynamically by the Matchmaker service. Deployments and updates strictly follow GitOps practices, ensuring automated, zero-downtime releases without manual code execution on production.
2. Problem Statement What’s the Problem? Traditional multiplayer game server architectures rely on dedicated EC2 instance fleets running continuously 24/7. During off-peak hours or low traffic periods, game studios suffer from massive idle compute costs. Furthermore, manual code deployments present severe operational risks, such as match interruptions, long deployment windows, and complex manual rollbacks. Keeping game server ports permanently open to the internet also exposes infrastructure to security threats and DDoS attacks.
The Solution This architecture follows a core design rule: Serverless for everything except live game sessions.
Low-latency metagame tasks (Auth, Asset Downloads, Matchmaking, Analytics) are handed off to AWS Serverless services (Cognito, API Gateway, Lambda, DynamoDB). Live game sessions run on an EC2 Spot Instance fleet (Graviton ARM64) inside a private/public VPC structure. Access to game servers is protected by dynamic Security Group rules managed by the Matchmaker Lambda, opening ports only for active players during a match and closing them immediately afterwards. Deployment is fully automated via GitHub Actions and AWS CodeDeploy using GitOps principles. Benefits and Return on Investment (ROI) Up to 80% Cost Reduction: Eliminates idle server expenses by using EC2 Spot Instances combined with ARM64 Graviton processors, running compute only when matches occur. Zero Egress & NAT Costs: Eliminates NAT Gateway fees by routing internal Lambda traffic to DynamoDB and EC2 APIs via private VPC Endpoints. Automated Zero-Downtime Deployment: Blue/Green deployment via CodeDeploy allows gradual traffic shifting and instant rollback if a release fails, preventing match disruptions. Enhanced Security: Enforces strict trust boundaries, short-lived scoped IAM credentials for asset downloads, and dynamic Security Group management for game ports. 3. Solution Architecture The architecture is divided into four distinct execution flows, each featuring its own trigger mechanisms and security trust boundaries.
Architectural Flow Breakdown Flow C: GitOps Deployment Loop (CI/CD Pipeline) Runs strictly during deployment and never interferes with live, ongoing game sessions. Developers push code and Infrastructure as Code (IaC) to Git. The GitOps pipeline (GitHub Actions) builds artifacts and triggers AWS CodeDeploy. CodeDeploy gradually shifts traffic to the new AWS Lambda version alias and updates AMI/Launch Templates for the EC2 Auto Scaling Group (ASG) Spot fleet. Simultaneously, the pipeline uploads client builds, patches, and server bundles to an Amazon S3 asset bucket—serving as the unified artifact repository for both clients and EC2 instances. If a deployment error occurs, traffic shifts back automatically without interrupting running matchmaking services. Flow A: Player Auth & Asset Distribution Players log in via Cognito User Pool (A1) and receive a JWT (A2). Cognito Identity Pool exchanges the JWT for temporary, prefix-scoped IAM credentials (A3). Game clients use these temporary credentials to download assets directly from S3 (A4): client builds, patches, and launcher files. The JWT token is passed to Flow R (A5)—the single intersection point between Auth and Matchmaking—allowing API Gateway to authorize incoming requests. Flow R: Synchronous Matchmaking & Session Provisioning The client sends a matchmaking request via Amazon CloudFront (protected by AWS WAF) to Amazon API Gateway (R1). After Cognito Authorizer validates the JWT, Matchmaker Lambda (located in a private subnet) executes (R2): Writes match state to Amazon DynamoDB via a VPC Gateway Endpoint (R4). Calls the EC2 control plane via a private VPC Interface Endpoint (R3) to request a warm instance from the ASG Spot fleet (G1). Assigns a game room and configures dynamic Security Group rules specifically for the player’s IP address. Matchmaker Lambda returns the server IP and port to the client. The client connects directly to the game instance via Internet Gateway (G3). When ASG launches a new EC2 instance, User Data scripts execute at boot, using an IAM Instance Profile to pull the latest server binary, config, and patch from S3 (G4). The instance initializes and immediately accepts assigned players. Flow E: Asynchronous Post-Match Processing & Analytics Once a match concludes, results are written to DynamoDB. DynamoDB Streams automatically trigger a background Lambda function (E2) to capture post-match logs, calculate player stats, and push events to the analytics store (E3). This flow is completely decoupled from Flow R, ensuring post-match processing never impacts matchmaking latency. AWS Services Used Amazon Cognito: User Pool (Authentication) & Identity Pool (Authorization / Temp IAM Credentials). AWS WAF & Amazon CloudFront: Edge security, DDoS protection, and global request routing. Amazon API Gateway: Serverless HTTP API endpoint handling player requests. AWS Lambda: Executes Matchmaker logic, CodeDeploy traffic management, and background log processing. Amazon DynamoDB & DynamoDB Streams: Single-table design for match state & real-time log capturing. Amazon EC2 Spot Fleet (Graviton ARM64): Cost-effective, high-performance compute fleet for live game sessions. AWS CodeDeploy & GitHub Actions: Fully automated GitOps deployment pipeline. Amazon S3 & AWS KMS: Centralized asset repository with data-at-rest encryption. VPC Endpoints (Gateway & Interface): Private network connections avoiding public internet egress. 4. Technical Implementation Implementation Phases Phase 1: Architecture & Security Boundary Definition (Month 1)
Design VPC subnets, route tables, IAM roles, security group automation, and KMS keys. Phase 2: Core Serverless Metagame & Auth Setup (Month 1-2)
Implement Cognito User/Identity Pools, S3 asset bucket policies, API Gateway, and DynamoDB single-table schema. Phase 3: Matchmaker & EC2 Spot Automation (Month 2)
Develop Matchmaker Lambda in private subnets, configure ASG Launch Templates with Graviton ARM64, and create User Data boot scripts. Phase 4: GitOps CI/CD & Asynchronous Analytics (Month 3)
Set up GitHub Actions workflows, CodeDeploy Blue/Green deployment hooks, DynamoDB Streams for post-match processing, and conduct load testing. Technical Requirements Infrastructure as Code (IaC): AWS CDK / Terraform for full environment reproducibility. Game Server Build: Dockerized / binary game server compiled for Graviton (ARM64 Linux). Security Standards: TLS 1.3 in transit, KMS encryption at rest, principle of least privilege for IAM instance roles and temp user credentials. 5. Timeline & Milestones Month 1: System architecture design, VPC/Network topology setup, and IAM security boundaries. Month 2: Serverless matchmaking development, Cognito integration, and EC2 Spot ASG automation. Month 3: GitOps pipeline implementation, load & stress testing, performance tuning, and final deployment. 6. Budget Estimation & Cost Optimization Cost Breakdown Highlights Zero Idle Compute: EC2 instances run ONLY during active matches. Matchmaking and Auth cost fractions of a cent via AWS Lambda & DynamoDB On-Demand. 70-90% Discount via Spot & Graviton: Graviton ARM64 Spot Instances provide industry-leading price-to-performance ratio for game servers. Elimination of NAT Gateway Fees: Matchmaker Lambda communicates with AWS services inside private subnets using free/low-cost VPC Endpoints instead of expensive NAT Gateway data transfer. Thin AMI Maintenance: Game binaries and patches are pulled dynamically from S3 at boot time, eliminating the overhead and cost of rebaking AMIs for minor game patches. 7. Risk Assessment Risk Item Impact Probability Mitigation Strategy Spot Instance Interruption Medium Low ASG maintains a small warm pool and uses multi-AZ Spot allocation strategies for instant replacement. Deployment Failure High Low AWS CodeDeploy performs gradual traffic shifting with automated rollback if health checks fail. Unauthorized Game Access High Low API Gateway validates JWT tokens; dynamic SG rules restrict game server access solely to authenticated player IPs during match windows. 8. Expected Outcomes Scalable Architecture: Seamlessly handles spikes from 10 to 10,000+ concurrent players without manual intervention. Extreme Cost Efficiency: Reduces operational cloud bills by up to 80% compared to traditional 24/7 dedicated server setups. Enterprise Security & Reliability: Enforces strict network boundaries, GitOps deployment safety, and automated post-match analytics processing.

title: Blog 2
content:
PROVISIONING ORACLE DATABASE@AWS RESOURCES USING TERRAFORM This blog explores Oracle Database@AWS, a co-location service that brings Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI) database services directly into AWS data centers. This solution allows companies to run enterprise-grade Oracle Exadata database workloads inside AWS with sub-millisecond latency connection to AWS services like Amazon EC2.
Key Architectural Concepts: Customer VPC & Application Layer: The client applications run on Amazon EC2 instances inside a standard AWS Customer VPC. Oracle Database (ODB) Network: An ODB Network VPC is established inside the AWS Region, housing client and backup subnets. It connects to the Customer VPC via ODB Peering. OCI Child Site (Co-located inside AWS Datacenter): The OCI Child site contains the actual physical Exadata Infrastructure running OCI Virtual Cloud Network (VCN) client and backup subnets mapped directly to AWS subnets. Control Plane & Automation: Resource orchestration and lifecycle management are automated via OCI Automation connected to the OCI Control Plane in the OCI Parent Region. Infrastructure as Code (IaC) with Terraform: By using the Terraform OCI and AWS providers, cloud engineers can provision this entire cross-cloud infrastructure in a unified workflow:
Create VPCs, Subnets, and Route Tables in AWS. Initialize OCI provider to provision the Exadata Infrastructure. Configure the private connectivity and network peering between AWS and OCI child sites automatically. Architecture Diagram: Links and References: Facebook Post: AWS Study Group Facebook Post Reference Article: Provision Oracle Database and AWS resources using Terraform

title: Event 2
content: Summary Report: AWS SageMaker Deep Dive Event Information: Event Name: AWS SageMaker Deep Dive (Highlighting features from re:Invent 2024) Date: June 27, 2026 Location: Hybrid (In-person & Virtual) at 26th Floor (and 36th Floor), Bitexco Financial Tower, 02 Hai Trieu Street, Ben Nghe Ward, District 1, Ho Chi Minh City Organizer: AWS (Amazon Web Services), broadcasted on AWS Study Group YouTube channel Event Objectives: Deep Dive into AI/ML Development: Explore the new SageMaker Unified Studio and the latest machine learning capabilities within the AWS ecosystem. Unified Development Experience: Showcase a single, integrated environment that unifies data processing, SQL analytics, ML model development, generative AI application building, and data governance. Break Down Silos: Enable Data Engineers, Data Scientists, and Data Analysts to collaborate in a single shared digital workspace. Speakers: Anh Trung – Speaker 1 Kirara Lador – Speaker 2 Isaac – Speaker 3 Key Highlights: 1. Overcoming Legacy Data Silos In the past, data processing tools (EMR, Glue, Athena) and ML model management tools were separated, causing constant switching overhead. Data sharing within organizations was bottlenecked by manual administrative approvals. Technical raw schemas made datasets incomprehensible to business users. 2. The Solution: SageMaker Unified Studio Unified Studio: A single pane of glass offering full access to code repositories, compute configurations, open-source AI models, and shared data catalogs. 3. Technology & Tooling Showcase Jupyter Lab IDE: Multi-language notebooks supporting Python, Scala, and SQL cells running seamlessly side-by-side. Includes Visual ETL capabilities for drag-and-drop pipelines. Integrated Amazon Q: Serves as a coding assistant inside the IDE to generate SQL queries from natural language, explain code blocks, debug errors, and refactor scripts. AI/ML Foundation Models: Integration with Amazon Bedrock, SageMaker Jumpstart, and advanced LLMs such as Amazon Nova and Claude 4. Introduces SageMaker HyperPod for distributed, large-scale training. Data Governance: Built-in Data Catalog, Data Lineage tracking, and packaging datasets as “Data Products.” 4. Live Demos & Case Studies Three-Role Collaboration Demo: Kiara demonstrated a workflow acting as a Data Engineer setting security rules, a Data Scientist requesting access to train a loan default prediction model, and a Data Analyst searching for Marketing data. The entire requesting and approval workflow was frictionless and automated. AI Business Metadata Generation: Using generative AI to automatically generate business descriptions for hundreds of technical table columns. Data Products Concept: Packaging related tables into one “Data Product” so users request access to one item rather than hundreds of tables separately. Key Takeaways: 1. Design Mindset & Data Collaboration Shift from fragmented workflows to active data collaboration. Data governance is not about locking data away, but making it easily discoverable while preserving security boundaries. Change Management: Utilizing Data Lineage to identify downstream “Data consumers” before altering database columns to prevent breaking other departments’ dashboards. 2. Technical Learnings Understood Data Lineage tracking to trace data ancestry from raw ingestion to final tables. Explored running Apache Airflow workflows (MWAA) directly inside the Unified Studio interface. Security & Best Practices: Implementing Column-level security to mask PII (such as employer names in credit datasets) before provisioning access to Data Scientists. Refactoring: Leveraging Amazon Q to refactor notebook cells into structured, clean code. Applying to Work: Self-Service Permissions: Implement UI-based permission request flows instead of manual GRANT SQL queries to eliminate database administrator bottlenecks. Dev Workflow Automation: Experiment with Amazon Q to generate Visual ETL pipelines from prompts, and use SageMaker Jumpstart to deploy open-source models with minimal code. Data Standardization: Transition away from manual CSV file transfers in S3 between Data Engineers and Data Scientists; introduce a standardized Business Glossary. Event Experience: Practical Value: Highly valuable lecture flow detailing data processing, IDE tools, AI integration, and live demos by Kiara and Isaac. Hands-on Workshop: Attendees participated in workshop lab sessions to interact with datasets directly. Networking & Mentorship: Speakers were highly accessible to discuss advanced architectures like SageMaker HyperPod setup after sessions. Memorable Highlight: The integration of the Spark debugging UI directly into the IDE without needing complex networking setups. Key Lessons & Next Steps: SageMaker Evolution: Amazon SageMaker has evolved from a machine learning service into a complete Machine Learning and Analytics platform covering the full lifecycle from raw data ingestion to GenAI application deployment. Next Steps: Deep dive into Data Lakehouse concepts, experiment with fast/lightweight Amazon Nova models, and integrate MLflow to track and log model training runs. Event Photos:

title: Week 2 Worklog
content: Week 2 Objectives: Learn about Amazon Elastic Compute Cloud (EC2) and virtual machines. Understand key concepts: Instance Types, Amazon Machine Images (AMI), Key Pairs, and Elastic IPs. Learn Security Groups for firewall rules. Practice deploying a simple web application on an EC2 instance. Get introduced to high availability concepts: Elastic Load Balancing (ELB) and Auto Scaling. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material Mon - Study EC2 architecture, Instance Types, and family classifications (T-family, M-family, etc.) 05/11/2026 05/11/2026 Amazon EC2 Instance Types Tue - Understand AMIs (Amazon Machine Images), Key Pairs, and how to allocate/associate Elastic IPs 05/12/2026 05/12/2026 Amazon Machine Images (AMI) Wed - Learn how to configure Security Groups - Practice: Launch an EC2 instance and connect via SSH (using PuTTY/Terminal) 05/13/2026 05/13/2026 Securely connect to EC2 Thu - Practice: Set up Nginx/Apache web server on EC2 and host a simple “Hello World” HTML page 05/14/2026 05/14/2026 Host Web Server on EC2 Fri - Study Elastic Load Balancer (ALB, NLB) and Auto Scaling Groups (ASG) theory 05/15/2026 05/15/2026 ELB & Auto Scaling Week 2 Achievements: Gained deep understanding of AWS virtual machines (EC2) and how they scale. Created and launched an EC2 Instance using Amazon Linux AMI. Mastered network access security using Security Groups (opening ports 22, 80, 443). Successfully deployed Nginx web server and hosted a static website on EC2, accessible via public IP. Learned how to scale compute resources using ELB and Auto Scaling Groups for fault tolerance.

title: Clean Up CloudFront & WAF
content: Step-by-Step Implementation Step 1: Disable and Delete CloudFront Distribution Go to the Amazon CloudFront console -> Distributions. Select the Distribution you created -> Click Disable. Confirm and wait 5-10 minutes for status transition to completed. Select it again and click Delete. Step 2: Disassociate and Delete WAF Web ACL Go to the AWS WAF & Shield console -> Web ACLs. Select your ACL (e.g. CreatedByCloudFront-56a8180e). Go to Associated AWS resources and disassociate the CloudFront distribution. Go back to Web ACLs list -> click Delete.

title: Configure ASG Warm Pool & S3 Bucket
content: Step-by-Step Implementation Step 1: Configure Auto Scaling Group (ASG) Warm Pool Create Auto Scaling Group: FightingGameServerASG. Attach Launch Template. Configure Warm Pool settings to maintain pre-initialized warm instances ready for instant match assignment. Step 2: Create Amazon S3 Asset Bucket & Policy Create S3 Bucket for client builds, patches, and game server bundles. Configure Public Read Bucket Policy and CORS rules. Step 3: Assign IAM Instance Profile Create FightingGameServerInstanceRole with S3 read permissions. Attach IAM Instance Profile to EC2 Launch Template.

title: Create CodeDeploy App & Execute Pipeline
content: Step-by-Step Implementation Step 1: Create AWS CodeDeploy Application & Deployment Group Create CodeDeploy Role for EC2 and Lambda deployments. Create CodeDeploy Application: FightingGameCodeDeployApp. Create Deployment Group targeting EC2 Spot instances and Lambda alias traffic shifting. Step 2: Execute Automated Deployment & Verify Logs Trigger GitHub Actions workflow execution. Monitor CodeDeploy job execution progress and verify deployment history.

title: Matchmaker Lambda & API Gateway REST API
content:
Overview In this chapter, you will build the FightingGameMatchmaker AWS Lambda function to process player queueing (POST /join) and match checks (GET /check), and expose these endpoints via Amazon API Gateway secured by Cognito Authorizer.
Sub-modules 5.3.1 Build Matchmaker Lambda Function 5.3.2 Deploy REST API & Cognito Authorizer

title: Verify User Registration UI
content: Step-by-Step Implementation Step 1: Open Frontend Game Client Launch the game client web interface. Enter username, email, and password to test account registration. Verify Cognito User Pool receives and stores the new user record.

title: Blog 3
content:
BUILDING AN INNOVATION SANDBOX ON AWS WITH REAL-TIME ANALYTICS DASHBOARD This article outlines how to create a secure, scalable Innovation Sandbox environment on AWS, combined with a self-service real-time analytics dashboard powered by Amazon Q Business. This solution enables organizations to host hackathons, training bootcamps, and R&D sandbox environments efficiently while maintaining centralized governance.
Core Architectural Features: Self-Service Dashboard & Amazon Q Business: Executives and participants can access a modern web dashboard hosted via Amazon CloudFront and Amazon S3 static assets. Users can request a custom generative AI web experience. This query is routed through Amazon API Gateway and AWS Lambda to invoke Amazon Q Business, returning a secure, anonymous web experience URL. Account Provisioning & Organizations: Managed inside an AWS Management Account using AWS Control Tower and AWS Organizations. Accounts are automatically provisioned under the InnovationSandbox Organizational Unit (OU) using CloudFormation templates. Sandbox account allocation data is kept in S3. Automated Deployment and Data Sync: Deployed via AWS CDK and local Git pipelines. Python scripts handle S3 data syncing, Amazon Q index synchronization, and CloudFront cache invalidation. Architectural Benefits: Centralized Governance: AWS Control Tower ensures guardrails are automatically applied to every new sandbox account. Generative AI Assistant: Amazon Q Business acts as a smart companion, answering participants’ technical queries in real-time. Infrastructure Automation: AWS CDK and CloudFormation automate account provisioning and dashboard deployments, removing administrative bottlenecks. Architecture Diagram: Links and References: Facebook Post: AWS Study Group Facebook Post Reference Article: Innovation Sandbox on AWS with Real-Time Analytics Dashboard

title: Week 3 Worklog
content: Week 3 Objectives: Learn about AWS storage options: Block Storage (EBS), Shared File Storage (EFS), Object Storage (S3), and Archival Storage (Glacier). Understand how to manage S3 Buckets and Objects. Practice hosting a Static Website on Amazon S3. Configure S3 features: Versioning, Lifecycle Policies, and Access Control Lists (ACLs). Understand data backup and restore mechanisms. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material Mon - Study storage types: EBS (Elastic Block Store) vs EFS (Elastic File System) vs S3 (Simple Storage Service) 05/18/2026 05/18/2026 AWS Storage Comparison Tue - Learn Amazon S3 fundamentals (buckets, objects, keys, global namespace) 05/19/2026 05/19/2026 Amazon S3 Overview Wed - Practice: Create S3 Buckets, upload files, and configure Bucket Policies and Access Control Lists (ACL) 05/20/2026 05/20/2026 S3 Access Control Thu - Practice: Deploy a static website on Amazon S3 and access it via the S3 endpoint URL 05/21/2026 05/21/2026 Host Static Site on S3 Fri - Practice: Enable S3 Versioning, configure Lifecycle Policies to transition data to Glacier, and review backups 05/22/2026 05/22/2026 S3 Lifecycle Management Week 3 Achievements: Gained clear knowledge of when to use EBS, EFS, and S3 for different application needs. Created S3 Buckets and successfully managed object storage permissions. Configured and deployed a public-facing static website hosted purely on S3. Configured Object Versioning to track file changes and prevent accidental deletion. Implemented Lifecycle rules to automatically transition older, infrequently-accessed data to Amazon S3 Glacier to optimize storage costs.

title: Blogs Posted
content:
During my internship, I actively shared technical insights and architectural case studies with the community. Below is the list of technical blogs I authored and posted on the AWS Study Group community page:
Blog 1 - HOW ALS GEOANALYTICS’ LITHOLENS REVOLUTIONIZES CORE LOGGING THROUGH MACHINE LEARNING WITH AMAZON EKS This blog explores how ALS Geoanalytics utilized Amazon EKS to orchestrate containerized Machine Learning inference workloads for their geological core logging system, optimizing performance, cost-efficiency, and scalability.
Blog 2 - PROVISIONING ORACLE DATABASE@AWS RESOURCES USING TERRAFORM This blog discusses the Oracle Database@AWS co-location offering that links OCI Exadata DB infrastructure inside AWS datacenters with sub-millisecond latencies, and how to automate the provisioning of this multi-cloud architecture using Terraform.
Blog 3 - BUILDING AN INNOVATION SANDBOX ON AWS WITH REAL-TIME ANALYTICS DASHBOARD This blog presents a secure, scalable AWS Innovation Sandbox framework utilizing AWS Control Tower and AWS Organizations for account provisioning, combined with a self-service real-time dashboard powered by Amazon Q Business.

title: EC2 Game Server & Auto Scaling Group Warm Pool

title: Week 4 Worklog
content: Week 4 Objectives: Learn about Amazon Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) and AWS networking. Understand networking components: Subnets, Route Tables, Internet Gateways (IGW), and NAT Gateways. Compare Security Groups (Stateful) vs Network Access Control Lists (NACL - Stateless) security layers. Design a hybrid network architecture featuring both Public and Private subnets. Practice building a complete VPC environment from scratch. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material Mon - Study VPC core concepts: CIDR block allocation, IP range design, and Subnetting 05/25/2026 05/25/2026 Amazon VPC Core Concepts Tue - Learn about Route Tables, Internet Gateways (IGW), and how traffic flows from public resources 05/26/2026 05/26/2026 VPC Route Tables Wed - Study NAT Gateways and how private subnets connect securely to the Internet 05/27/2026 05/27/2026 Amazon VPC NAT Gateways Thu - Compare and configure Security Groups vs Network ACLs (NACL) security rules 05/28/2026 05/28/2026 VPC Security Layer Comparison Fri - Practice: Construct a custom VPC with Public Subnets, Private Subnets, IGW, Route Tables, and launch EC2s inside 05/29/2026 05/29/2026 Build a Custom VPC Guide Week 4 Achievements: Gained a solid foundation in cloud networking, CIDR design, and subnet planning. Designed and built a custom VPC with isolated public (web) and private (database) zones. Configured Internet Gateways and Route Tables to enable external connectivity for public servers. Deployed a NAT Gateway in the public subnet to allow servers in private subnets to download system updates securely without internet exposure. Implemented double-layered security combining Security Groups (instance-level) and NACLs (subnet-level).

title: Workshop
content:
Live-Service Game Backend Architecture on AWS Overview This workshop provides a complete, step-by-step hands-on implementation guide for building a serverless and Spot Instance backend architecture for live-service multiplayer games on AWS.
Demo Web Link: http://fighting-game-assets-508768431157.s3-website-ap-southeast-1.amazonaws.com/ Source Code Link: https://github.com/Nothingtoread/fighting-game/tree/main Table of Contents 5.1 User Authentication with Amazon Cognito 5.1.1 Create Cognito User Pool 5.1.2 Configure App Client & Identity Pool 5.1.3 Verify User Registration UI 5.2 Database Setup: DynamoDB 5.2.1 Create MatchmakingQueue Table 5.2.2 Create ActiveMatches Table 5.3 Matchmaker Lambda & API Gateway REST API 5.3.1 Build Matchmaker Lambda Function 5.3.2 Deploy REST API & Cognito Authorizer 5.4 EC2 Game Server & Auto Scaling Group Warm Pool 5.4.1 Launch EC2 Base & Setup Node.js Server 5.4.2 Bake AMI & Create Launch Template 5.4.3 Configure ASG Warm Pool & S3 Bucket 5.5 GitOps CI/CD Pipeline & AWS CodeDeploy 5.5.1 Setup GitHub OIDC Provider & IAM Roles 5.5.2 Install CodeDeploy Agent on Ubuntu 24.04 5.5.3 Create CodeDeploy App & Execute Pipeline 5.6 Asynchronous Post-Match Processing 5.6.1 Enable DynamoDB Streams & IAM Roles 5.6.2 Create MatchAnalytic Lambda & Verify Logs 5.7 Resource Cleanup 5.7.1 Clean Up Cognito & DynamoDB 5.7.2 Clean Up Lambda & API Gateway 5.7.3 Clean Up CloudFront & WAF

title: Week 5 Worklog
content: Week 5 Objectives: Learn about database services on AWS: Relational (Amazon RDS) and Non-Relational (Amazon DynamoDB). Understand the core architectural differences between SQL (Relational) and NoSQL (Document/Key-Value) databases. Practice provisioning an Amazon RDS MySQL instance. Connect an application hosted on an EC2 instance securely to the RDS database. Configure backup and restore procedures on RDS. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material Mon - Study relational database architecture on AWS: Amazon RDS features, Multi-AZ, and Read Replicas 06/01/2026 06/01/2026 Amazon RDS Overview Tue - Study NoSQL databases: Amazon DynamoDB concepts, primary keys, indexes, and write capacity units 06/02/2026 06/02/2026 Amazon DynamoDB Guide Wed - Practice: Provision a MySQL database instance in Amazon RDS inside the private subnet 06/03/2026 06/03/2026 Create an RDS MySQL DB Thu - Practice: Configure security groups and connect an EC2 instance to the RDS instance via MySQL client 06/04/2026 06/04/2026 Connect EC2 to RDS MySQL Fri - Practice: Perform manual snapshots, set up automated backups, and practice database recovery 06/05/2026 06/05/2026 RDS Backups and Restores Week 5 Achievements: Mastered SQL vs NoSQL differences and learned when to use RDS vs DynamoDB. Created a private database subnet group and successfully deployed an RDS MySQL instance inside a private subnet. Set up secure EC2-to-RDS communication by restricting RDS Security Group access rules exclusively to the EC2 security group. Populated tables, executed SQL queries from EC2, and verified data storage. Configured automated nightly backups and successfully restored database data from a manual backup snapshot.

content:

title: Self-Assessment
content:
During my internship at Amazon Web Services Vietnam Company Limited from 05/05/2026 to 30/07/2026, I had a valuable opportunity to learn, practice, and apply cloud computing concepts in a professional environment.
I participated in the Workforce Bootcamp - First Cloud AI Journey, through which I gained hands-on experience with core AWS services, VPC networking, storage management, databases, Infrastructure as Code using Terraform, and application containerization with Docker and ECS. I also improved my technical documentation and research skills by writing architectural blog posts.
In terms of work ethic, I consistently aimed to complete lab assignments on time, adhered to program guidelines, and actively discussed technical problems with peers to find solutions.
To objectively reflect on my internship period, I evaluate myself at a Fair level overall, with the following criteria breakdown:
No. Criteria Description Good Fair Average 1 Professional knowledge & skills Understanding of the field, applying knowledge in practice, proficiency with tools, work quality ☐ ✅ ☐ 2 Ability to learn Ability to absorb new knowledge and learn quickly ✅ ☐ ☐ 3 Proactiveness Taking initiative, seeking out tasks without waiting for instructions ☐ ✅ ☐ 4 Sense of responsibility Completing tasks on time and ensuring quality ✅ ☐ ☐ 5 Discipline Adhering to schedules, rules, and work processes ☐ ✅ ☐ 6 Progressive mindset Willingness to receive feedback and improve oneself ✅ ☐ ☐ 7 Communication Presenting ideas and reporting work clearly ☐ ✅ ☐ 8 Teamwork Working effectively with colleagues and participating in teams ☐ ✅ ☐ 9 Professional conduct Respecting colleagues, partners, and the work environment ✅ ☐ ☐ 10 Problem-solving skills Identifying problems, proposing solutions, and showing creativity ☐ ☐ ✅ 11 Contribution to project/team Work effectiveness, innovative ideas, recognition from the team ☐ ✅ ☐ 12 Overall General evaluation of the entire internship period ☐ ✅ ☐ Areas for Improvement: Strengthen independent troubleshooting skills when encountering complex permission (IAM) or network routing issues. Enhance time management to better balance reading reference documentation and doing hands-on configurations. Be more proactive in voicing ideas and participating in discussions during team meetings.

title: Week 6 Worklog
content: Week 6 Objectives: Learn about Serverless computing on AWS using AWS Lambda. Study API routing using Amazon API Gateway. Understand messaging and event services: Amazon SNS, Amazon SQS, and Amazon EventBridge. Practice building a REST API backend with AWS Lambda. Integrate API Gateway with Lambda functions and test/monitor the API. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material Mon - Study AWS Lambda fundamentals (serverless architecture, execution environment, triggers, cold starts) 06/08/2026 06/08/2026 AWS Lambda Developer Guide Tue - Study Amazon API Gateway (REST vs HTTP APIs, integration types, CORS configuration) 06/09/2026 06/09/2026 Amazon API Gateway Guide Wed - Learn Event-driven integration tools: SQS (Queues), SNS (Notification Pub/Sub), and EventBridge (Cron/Events) 06/10/2026 06/10/2026 AWS Serverless Applications Thu - Practice: Create a Python/NodeJS Lambda function and configure an API Gateway trigger 06/11/2026 06/11/2026 Build API Gateway with Lambda Fri - Practice: Deploy a GET/POST REST endpoint, test payload delivery, and track logs in CloudWatch Logs 06/12/2026 06/12/2026 Monitor Lambda with CloudWatch Week 6 Achievements: Gained a clear understanding of Serverless design patterns and event-driven architectures. Developed serverless code inside Lambda functions using Python/NodeJS. Built and published a functional REST API backend using Amazon API Gateway. Successfully integrated API Gateway proxy endpoints to trigger Lambda functions. Mastered testing APIs using tools like Postman/cURL, and diagnosed serverless performance logs in Amazon CloudWatch. Explored decoupling microservices using SQS (queuing) and SNS (pub-sub).

title: Sharing and Feedback
content:
Here are my personal reflections and constructive feedback after completing my journey with the First Cloud AI Journey (FCAJ) program:
1. Learning & Working Environment The program provides an incredibly dynamic and collaborative ecosystem for students nationwide who are passionate about cloud technology. While the learning curve was steep and the pace was fast initially, the welcoming community made it easy to adapt. Although mostly virtual, discussions on Slack and Discord were highly active, and peers and coordinators were always ready to assist with technical blockers.
2. Guidance from Mentors & Administrative Support I highly appreciate the mentoring style in this program. Instead of simply providing the solutions to configuration bugs, my mentor guided me through reading error logs, searching official AWS documentation, and troubleshooting independently. This approach has greatly improved my analytical thinking. The administrative team was also very supportive, ensuring smooth communications and quick handling of programmatic requirements.
3. Relevance to My Major (Machine Learning & Applications) As a student majoring in Machine Learning and Applications at Ho Chi Minh City University of Technology (HUTECH), this training was the perfect bridge. I used to write machine learning models locally; now, through FCAJ, I understand how to deploy and scale them on AWS using Serverless microservices, Docker packaging, and container orchestration on Amazon ECS/Fargate.
4. What I Found Most Satisfying Direct access to real AWS Cloud resources to practice labs and build actual infrastructure projects. Huge growth in self-learning habits and structured technical writing skills. Building a network of talented peers who share similar career goals in Cloud and DevOps. 5. Suggestions for Future Cohorts More Live Troubleshooting Labs: It would be highly beneficial to introduce a few live lab sessions with mentors focused on fixing common configuration mistakes (e.g., IAM permission errors or VPC subnet routing blocks). Social Sessions: Organizing brief virtual meetups or informal coffee chats would help participants connect more closely beyond purely technical tasks. 6. Conclusion If a fellow student asked me about joining the First Cloud AI Journey, I would recommend it without hesitation. It is a fantastic bridge to cover the gap between academic theory and real-world engineering demands.

title: Week 7 Worklog
content: Week 7 Objectives: Learn about Infrastructure as Code (IaC) benefits and tools. Understand HashiCorp Terraform syntax, State management, Providers, Resources, Variables, and Modules. Study AWS CloudFormation as a native IaC alternative. Practice writing Terraform configuration files to automate the creation of network (VPC), compute (EC2), and storage (S3) resources. Learn how to manage infrastructure lifecycle using IaC. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material Mon - Learn IaC benefits and Terraform syntax (HCL - HashiCorp Configuration Language) 06/15/2026 06/15/2026 Terraform Introduction Tue - Study Terraform workflow: init, plan, apply, and destroy commands; learn State file significance 06/16/2026 06/16/2026 Terraform CLI Commands Wed - Study AWS CloudFormation: templates, JSON/YAML structure, Stacks, and nested templates 06/17/2026 06/17/2026 AWS CloudFormation Overview Thu - Practice: Write Terraform code to deploy a custom VPC with public subnets and an Internet Gateway 06/18/2026 06/18/2026 AWS Provider for Terraform Fri - Practice: Extend Terraform files to provision an EC2 instance and an S3 bucket; deploy and verify resources 06/19/2026 06/19/2026 Terraform AWS Tutorials Week 7 Achievements: Gained a solid grasp of Declarative Infrastructure management vs imperative scripting. Installed Terraform CLI on local workstation and configured the AWS provider credentials. Mastered writing declarative .tf configuration files including variables, outputs, and local state handling. Successfully initialized, planned, and applied a configuration to provision a VPC, Route Tables, an EC2 Web Server, and an S3 bucket in one click. Learned how to update and clean up cloud resources dynamically using terraform apply and terraform destroy.

title: Week 8 Worklog
content: Week 8 Objectives: Learn Docker containerization concepts. Practice creating Dockerfiles, building Docker Images, and running Docker Containers locally. Understand Amazon Elastic Container Registry (ECR) for hosting private container images. Learn about Amazon Elastic Container Service (ECS) and serverless container hosting via AWS Fargate. Deploy a containerized web application on Amazon ECS using AWS Fargate. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material Mon - Study Containerization theory vs Virtualization; install Docker Desktop 06/22/2026 06/22/2026 Docker Get Started Guide Tue - Learn Dockerfile instructions (FROM, RUN, COPY, EXPOSE, CMD) - Practice: Package a simple web application into a Docker Image 06/23/2026 06/23/2026 Dockerfile Reference Wed - Study Amazon ECR (Elastic Container Registry) repositories - Practice: Authenticate, tag, and push the local Docker image to ECR 06/24/2026 06/24/2026 Amazon ECR Overview Thu - Learn ECS (Elastic Container Service) core components: clusters, task definitions, services, and EC2 vs Fargate launch types 06/25/2026 06/25/2026 Amazon ECS Architecture Fri - Practice: Create an ECS cluster, write a Task Definition pointing to ECR image, and deploy a Service using AWS Fargate 06/26/2026 06/26/2026 Deploy Container on ECS Fargate Week 8 Achievements: Acquired Docker development skills, including image layering, container caching, and port mapping. Containerized a Node.js/Python web application by writing a custom multi-stage Dockerfile. Created an Amazon ECR repository and successfully pushed container images using the AWS CLI. Mastered ECS cluster setup and configured ECS Task Definitions with specific CPU and Memory resource limits. Successfully launched a serverless containerized service via AWS Fargate and validated web application access through its public IP.

title: Week 9 Worklog
content: Week 9 Objectives: Learn about systems monitoring and logging using Amazon CloudWatch. Understand audit trails and governance tracking via AWS CloudTrail. Study encryption and credential management tools: AWS Key Management Service (KMS) and AWS Secrets Manager. Set up metrics, dashboards, and CloudWatch Alarms. Secure applications by storing database passwords inside Secrets Manager. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material Mon - Study Amazon CloudWatch architecture (metrics, logs, alarms, dashboards) 06/29/2026 06/29/2026 Amazon CloudWatch Guide Tue - Study AWS CloudTrail: configuring trails, checking activity logs, and tracking API calls 06/30/2026 06/30/2026 AWS CloudTrail Overview Wed - Study AWS Key Management Service (KMS) and AWS Secrets Manager for security governance 07/01/2026 07/01/2026 AWS Secrets Manager Introduction Thu - Practice: Configure CloudWatch Alarms to monitor EC2 CPU usage (alert if > 80%) and create a simple monitoring dashboard 07/02/2026 07/02/2026 Create CloudWatch Alarm Fri - Practice: Store database secrets in AWS Secrets Manager and retrieve them programmatically 07/03/2026 07/03/2026 Retrieve Secret via Code Week 9 Achievements: Gained comprehensive knowledge of AWS auditing, monitoring, and compliance structures. Configured custom CloudWatch Dashboards visualizing CPU utilization and network activity. Successfully set up an active CloudWatch Alarm with email notification (via SNS) when EC2 CPU exceeds 80%. Monitored management and data events using CloudTrail to audit administrative actions. Eliminated hardcoded passwords by migrating database credentials to AWS Secrets Manager, accessing them securely via IAM permissions.

title: Week 10 Worklog
content: Week 10 Objectives: Learn DevOps practices on AWS using CI/CD pipeline services: AWS CodePipeline, AWS CodeBuild, and AWS CodeDeploy. Study the 6 pillars of the AWS Well-Architected Framework. Practice building an automated CI/CD pipeline. Integrate GitHub with AWS to trigger automatic application deployment. Conduct a comprehensive program review of all learned topics. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material Mon - Study AWS developer tools: CodePipeline (orchestration), CodeBuild (build/test), and CodeDeploy (deployment) 07/06/2026 07/06/2026 AWS DevOps Developer Tools Tue - Study AWS Well-Architected Framework pillars - Practice: Create a CI/CD pipeline triggering automated code deployment from GitHub to AWS - Final review and project wrap-up 07/07/2026 07/07/2026 AWS Well-Architected Pillars Week 10 Achievements: Gained solid knowledge of continuous integration and continuous deployment (CI/CD) methodologies. Understood how to architect secure, high-performing, resilient, and efficient infrastructure using the AWS Well-Architected Framework. Successfully built an end-to-end deployment pipeline using AWS CodePipeline. Configured automated source triggers linking a GitHub repository to AWS, running builds and deploying static websites/applications automatically upon code push. Conducted a thorough final review, summarizing the entire 10-week AWS training program.

title: Week 11 Worklog
content: Week 11 Objectives: Analyze project requirements and finalize functional specifications. Design system architecture on AWS, ensuring scalability and security. Distribute responsibilities and set up team collaboration workflows. Provision initial AWS infrastructure and develop core system functionalities. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material Mon - Research and analyze topic requirements - Draft functional specifications and user stories 07/13/2026 07/13/2026 None Tue - Design system architecture diagram - Select AWS compute, database, and storage services 07/14/2026 07/14/2026 None Wed - Distribute development tasks among members - Set up GitHub repository & branching strategy 07/15/2026 07/15/2026 None Thu - Build AWS foundation infrastructure (VPC, Subnets, IAM) using Terraform 07/16/2026 07/16/2026 None Fri - Develop core backend/frontend features - Perform initial integration testing 07/17/2026 07/17/2026 None Week 11 Achievements: Completed detailed requirements analysis and scope definition. Finalized and approved the cloud-native system architecture diagram on AWS. Configured Git repository, CI/CD pipeline structures, and task boards for team members. Deployed the basic network layout and security policies (VPC, Security Groups) via Terraform. Successfully built and verified communication between the core components of the system.

title: Week 12 Worklog
content: Week 12 Objectives: Complete all remaining system functionalities. Conduct end-to-end system testing and performance/cost optimizations. Set up system monitoring and automated backup policies. Finalize technical documentation, project report slides, and demo recordings. Summarize the internship journey and lessons learned. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material Mon - Complete remaining backend and frontend tasks - Perform overall functional testing 07/20/2026 07/20/2026 None Tue - Optimize infrastructure performance and AWS service costs - Configure resource scaling limits 07/21/2026 07/21/2026 None Wed - Set up CloudWatch dashboards & alarms - Configure AWS Backup for databases and S3 buckets 07/22/2026 07/22/2026 None Thu - Finalize system technical architecture documents - Prepare slide presentation and demo video 07/23/2026 07/23/2026 None Fri - Project defense and demo presentation - Conduct internship wrap-up and self-evaluation 07/24/2026 07/24/2026 None Week 12 Achievements: Fully completed all functional and non-functional requirements of the group project. Successfully executed end-to-end load testing and adjusted AWS resources to save up to 25% budget. Established comprehensive cloud monitoring with CloudWatch and configured automated daily backups. Completed a thorough technical guide and documentation for project deployment and handover. Successfully presented the group project to the mentors and completed the 12-week bootcamp program.
